schools.summary: Henry makes it a lesson on provoking one another to good works: a free-will offering, not a tax, for God loves a cheerful giver, and encouragement due to Solomon, young and tender.
Ellicott glosses 'young and tender' and 'alone of his brothers,' and the rare word birah (palace), used of the Temple only here.
Clarke sets the scene and cites the Targum, 'the palace is not for a son of man, but for the name of the Word of the Lord God.'
Gill notes David turns from charging Solomon to addressing the whole congregation again.

themes: the house is God's not man's

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil unfold the catalogue of materials, the uncertain gems (onyx/shoham, puk, riqmah) and shayish, the white marble named only here.
Clarke identifies shayish as Parian marble and recalls Josephus on the temple of white polished marble.
Gill: David prepared with all his might, to the utmost of his ability.
Ellicott: the gift is David's own segullah (peculium), given 'over and above' the public store, the verb a present 'I give.'
Gill: David set his affection to the house of God, desiring it built in grand manner.

themes: over and above the public store

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott reckon the sums, locate Ophir's gold (Indian, from Abhira), explain the overlaying of the temple walls, and note the Syriac/Arabic variant 'a thousand thousand talents.'
Gill computes the value of the three thousand talents of gold (Scheuchzer, Brerewood).
Wesley: the gold of Ophir the best and purest, the walls overlaid with beaten silver plates.
Keil and Ellicott expound 'to fill his hand' = to provide oneself an offering to bring (Exodus 32:29), and the rare infinitive.
Clarke and Wesley read 'fill his hand' as bringing an offering; Wesley: those who serve God will have their hands full.
Gill glosses gold for gold, silver for silver.

themes: to fill the hand for the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott: the titles all render one word (sarim), 'rulers of the king's work' the stewards of the royal domains; Keil identifies the daric (adarkon) as a Persian coin, not a drachma.
Gill names the princes of the twelve tribes.
Ellicott sets the totals, identifies the daric, and argues the chronicler substituted a later coin-name (no coins before the exile); Keil records the amounts and the joy.
Clarke computes the vast sums of gold, silver, brass and iron.
Gill: given for the building, adorning, and the utensils of the house.

outliers.note: Reads 'drams' (darics) as an anachronism: no real coins are mentioned in Scripture before the exile, so the chronicler substituted a familiar Persian coin-name for an older, unknown expression of value.

schools.summary: Ellicott: the stones given to the treasure under the charge of Jehiel the Gershonite, as the princes gave at the tabernacle (Exodus 35:27).
Gill: precious stones like those named before.
Ellicott: the perfect heart, the exultation compared to Deborah's song (Judges 5:1).
Wesley: the willingness an effect of grace, a token of favour, and a pledge of the work's accomplishment; David's joy the reviving of a dying man.
Gill: they rejoiced not only that they had gifts but hearts to give.
Keil sets the thanksgiving as the close of David's last assembly; Ellicott parses 'before all,' 'Israel our father' (not 'Jehovah our father'), and 'from eternity to eternity.'
Henry: it becomes aged and dying saints to enlarge their hearts in praise, speaking the language of the world of everlasting praise.
Wesley echoes Henry: David full of days, nearer the land of everlasting praise, the more he speaks its language.
Gill: David ascribes both the ability and the willingness to God, who works to will and to do.
Ellicott: David arrogates nothing, ascribing all to God; glosses gedullah, prowess, splendour, 'victory'; the doxology of Revelation; Keil grounds it in 'all in heaven and earth is thine.'
Clarke gives the Targum's recital of God's mighty deeds (Egypt, Red Sea, Sinai, Amalek, sun at Gibeon).
Gill: God possesses all greatness, power and glory; men's grandeur and victories are all of God.

themes: the humility of ascribing all to God

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the riches and honour are from before thee'; God makes great and gives strength (1 Samuel 2:7-8; Luke 1:52).
Gill: riches and honour owe nothing to merit or industry but to God's providence.

themes: riches and honour come of God

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read modim as an enduring, ever-renewed praise; the present participles and the phrase 'name of thy glory,' found only here.
Gill: thanks that so great a God takes notice of them and bestows so much good.
Keil: man can give nothing of himself, 'from thy hand have we given'; Ellicott: the idiom 'keep strength' confined to Chronicles and Daniel, 'from thee is the whole.'
Wesley: God gives both the riches and the willing heart, so we only pay a debt; Clarke seals it with the Targum, 'of the blessings of thy hands have we given thee.'
Gill: who am I, dust and ashes, a sinful creature unworthy?

themes: of thine own have we given thee

schools.summary: Keil: Israel held only the usufruct, not the freehold; life a pilgrimage, days a shadow; Ellicott renders the last clause 'no hope.'
Wesley: we are tenants, not proprietors, giving what we must soon leave; Clarke: we lodge as for a night, 'no expectation' of exemption from decay.
Gill: Israel held the land as the Lord's, not in their own right.

debates.question: How is the final clause 'there is none abiding' to be rendered?

sides.position: 'there is none abiding' — no earthly form endures (Authorized rendering)
'there is no hope/expectation' (mikveh) — no assured or permanent future

themes: mortality as the ground of humility

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'all this store' a multitude of riches, a clearer restatement of v.14; Keil: all comes from the hand of God, with a note on the Kethib/Keri pronoun.
Wesley: acknowledge God in all spiritual things, referring every good thought, desire and work to his grace.
Gill retails the older reckonings of the vast store, tens of thousands of tons.

themes: the store comes of God's hand
God the source of every good

schools.summary: Keil: before the heart-searching God David pleads his and the people's willing uprightness; Ellicott: 'uprightness' is straightness, the offering without grudging or hypocrisy, for God's glory.
Gill: God searches and perfectly knows the heart, whether the deed proceeds from it.
Keil and Ellicott: a prayer that God preserve for ever the frame of the people's heart and direct it to himself (1 Samuel 7:3).
Clarke: the good dispositions came from God, continue them by grace; Wesley: 'prepare' is rather 'confirm,' carry on the good work by grace.
Gill: the covenant God of Abraham, Isaac and Israel, mindful of his promise.

themes: the frame of the heart preserved

schools.summary: Keil: a whole, undivided heart to keep and do the commands and build the temple; Ellicott: 'do all these things' = the whole law or all David's designs (Deuteronomy 6:17).
Gill: a perfect heart to keep all God's laws cordially and sincerely.

themes: the charge turned into a petition

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'now' is entreaty not time, a liturgical blessing, bowed and worshipped = prostrated, the king honoured as God's representative (1 Kings 1:31); Keil connects the paired verbs.
Clarke and Wesley distinguish the double homage: the Lord with religious, the king with civil worship.
Henry: only those benefit who join not by bowing the head but by lifting up the soul.
Gill names the congregation of princes, captains and officers.

themes: worship of God and homage to the king

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott describe the great feast: a thousand each of bullocks, rams and lambs as burnt-offerings, with libations, and thank-offerings (shelamim) for all Israel to share.
Clarke: the drink-offerings a thousand (Targum); the final sacrifices peace-offerings on whose flesh the people feasted.
Gill: David and the congregation sacrificed.
Keil and Ellicott: Solomon made king 'the second time' (first at 1 Chr 23:1 / 1 Kings 1:32), anointed 'to the LORD' = by his will; Zadok's priesthood a notice peculiar to Chronicles.
Clarke: the first anointing was in haste under Adonijah, now repeated with pomp; Wesley: anointing Zadok settled only the reversion, Abiathar still officiating.
Gill sets the feast before the ark in the tabernacle David had pitched.

debates.question: Did the anointing install Zadok as high priest at once, or only settle the reversion?

sides.position: Zadok received the high-priesthood then, with the consent of the estates
it only settled the reversion; Abiathar continued to officiate until Solomon deposed him

schools.summary: Keil: 'he prospered' fulfils David's hope, 'all Israel obeyed' = submitted; Ellicott: David did not abdicate, vv.23-25 are anticipative.
Henry: the throne called the LORD's because God was King of Israel (1 Samuel 12:12); Solomon's kingdom typifies the Messiah's (Psalms 2:6).
Gill: God gave and established Solomon on the throne as his vicegerent.
Keil: 'give the hand under' means submit, but not from an oath-custom; Ellicott: an ancient mode of homage, with a glance at Adonijah's attempt.
Clarke: they swore fealty, the hand under the thigh an oath-form (Genesis 24:9); Wesley: the throne is the LORD's, who founded and filled it.
Gill names the princes of the tribes and the officers of the army.

debates.question: What does 'gave the hand under Solomon' signify?

sides.position: a sworn oath of fealty (the hand-under-thigh oath-form)
simple submission or homage, no oath necessarily implied

themes: gave the hand under the king

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'put upon him a glory of kingship' none before had worn; only two or three kings had preceded (1 Kings 3:12).
Gill: God magnified Solomon by a large share of wisdom for government.
Keil and Ellicott: 'over all Israel' looks back to the earlier reign over Judah alone (2 Samuel 5:1ff.).
Henry: David's repentance as remarkable as his sin; even the chief of sinners may hope for a glorious departure through the Saviour's blood.
Wesley: the writer returns to close David's reign and life, leaving him asleep.
Gill: the reign long and glorious.

themes: grace makes a sinner's end illustrious

schools.summary: Keil confirms forty years (seven at Hebron, thirty-three at Jerusalem); Ellicott: a duplicate of 1 Kings 2:11, the seven years more exactly seven and a half (2 Samuel 5:5).
Gill leaves the numbers to speak, with only a cross-reference.

themes: seven years or seven and a half

schools.summary: Clarke: David had all his heart could wish, full of age, days, riches and honour; Wesley: he had enough of the world and knew it, willing to leave for a better place.
Ellicott: 'full of days' is 'satisfied with days' (Genesis 15:15); the closing formula (1 Kings 11:43).
Gill fixes the age at about seventy.

themes: died in a good old age
full of days, riches and honour
a peaceful end after a soldier's life

schools.summary: Ellicott: dibre, 'written on' a scroll (Gesenius, 'The Words of Samuel'); Keil: the acts recorded with 'the times' and the surrounding kingdoms (Philistia, Edom, Moab, Ammon, Aram).
Clarke: written by Samuel, Nathan and Gad, now lost save what Scripture keeps; Wesley: the prophets were also historiographers.
Gill: 'first and last,' all David's memorable acts before and during his reign.

themes: the times that went over him

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'might' is prowess (as v.11); 'the times that went over him' the seasons of fortune; the Syriac adds that David departed from nothing God commanded.
Clarke ends with Delaney's estimate of David, hero, penitent, poet and prophet, and the one blot of Uriah and Bathsheba answered by a deep repentance.
Gill: 'his might,' the battles fought and victories won.

themes: the one blot and the deeper repentance
